title: Quiz: Kernel Encoding
description: Test your understanding of quantum kernel concepts

questions.question: What is the quantum kernel k(x, x') mathematically?
questions.explanation: The quantum kernel is defined as k(x, x') = |⟨φ(x)|φ(x')⟩|², the squared magnitude of the overlap between quantum-encoded states. The absolute value squared gives a real, non-negative similarity measure.

questions.question: What is the dimension of the feature space for an n-qubit quantum feature map?
questions.explanation: An n-qubit quantum state lives in a 2ⁿ-dimensional Hilbert space. This exponential scaling is what gives quantum feature maps their potential power over classical methods.

questions.question: What makes the ZZ-feature map more expressive than simple product state encodings?
questions.explanation: The ZZ-feature map includes entangling gates (like CNOT) and ZZ-interaction terms (Rzz(x_i * x_j)) that create correlations between qubits. This entanglement allows access to a larger portion of the exponential Hilbert space.

questions.question: A quantum kernel matrix must be positive semi-definite.
questions.explanation: True. Any valid kernel function corresponds to an inner product in some feature space, which guarantees the kernel matrix is positive semi-definite. Quantum kernels satisfy this property since |⟨φ(x)|φ(x')⟩|² forms a valid kernel.

questions.question: What is a key computational cost of quantum kernel methods?

options.text: Exponential memory for storing quantum states
O(n²) kernel matrix evaluations for n training samples

questions.explanation: Building the kernel matrix requires computing k(xᵢ, xⱼ) for all pairs of training samples, which is O(n²) kernel evaluations. This can be expensive for large datasets, though each individual kernel evaluation uses quantum resources.
